title: PySpark Execution Model
subtitle: Test map — lazy evaluation, stages, shuffles, and slow-job diagnosis

nodes.label: Lazy Evaluation
nodes.definition: Spark does not run transformations immediately. It records a logical plan until an action forces execution.
nodes.whyItMatters: Lazy evaluation enables optimization and avoids wasted work, but delays when problems appear.
nodes.safeSentence: Transformations are lazy; nothing runs until an action triggers a job.
nodes.commonTrap: Assuming a transformation already ran because you called a DataFrame method.
nodes.interviewAnswer: Spark builds a plan first and executes only when an action is called, which lets Catalyst optimize before running.

nodes.label: Lineage DAG
nodes.definition: The recorded transformation graph from sources to the current DataFrame.
nodes.whyItMatters: Lineage explains what Spark will recompute and what can be recomputed after failure.
nodes.safeSentence: Lineage is the recipe Spark can replay if partitions are lost.
nodes.interviewAnswer: Lineage is the DAG of transformations; it supports fault tolerance and logical planning.

nodes.label: Transformations
nodes.definition: Operations that return a new DataFrame without triggering a cluster job.
nodes.whyItMatters: Most DataFrame code is transformations; they define the plan.
nodes.safeSentence: select, filter, withColumn, join, and groupBy are transformations — they extend the plan.
nodes.commonTrap: Treating join or groupBy as cheap because they return quickly in the driver.
nodes.interviewAnswer: Transformations are lazy and only describe the computation graph.

nodes.label: Actions
nodes.definition: Operations that trigger execution and return a result or side effect to the driver.
nodes.whyItMatters: Actions are where jobs start, metrics appear, and failures surface.
nodes.safeSentence: count, collect, show, and write are actions — they start jobs.
nodes.interviewAnswer: An action materializes the plan into stages and tasks on the cluster.

nodes.label: Narrow Transformations
nodes.definition: Each input partition maps to at most one output partition (map, filter, select).
nodes.whyItMatters: Narrow ops are cheap and usually avoid shuffle.
nodes.safeSentence: Narrow transformations keep partition count stable and stay local to each partition.

nodes.label: Wide Transformations
nodes.definition: Require data movement across partitions (groupBy, join, repartition).
nodes.whyItMatters: Wide transformations often cause shuffle — the main performance cliff.
nodes.safeSentence: Wide transformations usually need a shuffle to regroup data by key.
nodes.interviewAnswer: Joins and aggregations are wide; they redistribute data and are expensive at scale.

nodes.label: Stages and Tasks
nodes.definition: Spark splits a job into stages separated by shuffle boundaries; each stage has parallel tasks per partition.
nodes.whyItMatters: Stages explain parallelism and where barriers occur.
nodes.safeSentence: A shuffle boundary ends one stage and starts another; tasks run one partition at a time per stage.
nodes.interviewAnswer: Stages are shuffle-separated execution phases; tasks are partition-level units of work.

nodes.label: Shuffle
nodes.definition: Exchange of data across executors so rows with the same key land on the same partition.
nodes.whyItMatters: Shuffle is network- and disk-heavy; it dominates many slow jobs.
nodes.safeSentence: Shuffle redistributes data by key and is often the bottleneck in joins and aggregations.
nodes.commonTrap: Calling repartition or distinct without understanding the shuffle cost.
nodes.interviewAnswer: Shuffle happens when data must be regrouped; minimize it with better keys, filters, and partition strategy.

nodes.label: Partition Skew
nodes.definition: Some partitions hold far more data than others, leaving tasks straggling.
nodes.whyItMatters: Skew makes one task finish much later than the rest.
nodes.safeSentence: Skew means a few keys own most of the data; watch for one task running much longer in Spark UI.
nodes.interviewAnswer: I check stage task duration distribution; one long task often signals skew on a hot key.

nodes.label: Catalyst Optimizer
nodes.definition: Spark SQL engine that optimizes the logical plan (predicate pushdown, column pruning).
nodes.whyItMatters: Explains why filter-early and narrow projections improve performance without manual micro-tuning.
nodes.safeSentence: Catalyst can push filters and drop unused columns before expensive shuffles.

nodes.label: Spark UI
nodes.definition: Web UI showing jobs, stages, tasks, shuffle read/write, and SQL plans.
nodes.whyItMatters: First place to look when a job is slow or failing.
nodes.safeSentence: I start with Jobs → Stages → task skew, then SQL/DataFrame explain for the plan.

nodes.label: Slow Job Playbook
nodes.definition: Practical sequence: confirm action, inspect plan, find shuffle, check skew, reduce data early.
nodes.whyItMatters: Interview and production troubleshooting need a repeatable pattern.
nodes.safeSentence: I ask what action ran, where the shuffle is, whether skew exists, and what data could be filtered earlier.
nodes.interviewAnswer: I trace the action, inspect stages for shuffle volume, look for stragglers, then attack data volume and join keys.
relatedQuestions: Where is the shuffle and how large is it?
Is one task much slower than the others?

paths.label: Execution Lane
paths.description: From lazy plan to action, stages, and shuffle.

paths.label: Troubleshoot Lane
paths.description: Diagnose slow jobs with UI, skew, and a playbook.
